# 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--01--16_06:19:56-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 190 English News Articles published between 2026-01-09 and 2026-01-16.*

## 1. Sri Lanka shifts from rescue to rebuilding after Cyclone Ditwah

Colombo has moved fast from emergency relief to reconstruction, erecting a high-powered Presidential Task Force and launching Project 5M to finance tens of thousands of new homes. The Government has earmarked Rs. 500bn in 2026, kicked off house-building in Anuradhapura, and tapped grants and in‑kind aid from India, the UAE, the US, China, Brazil, Cyprus and the Sri Lankan diaspora. Fiscal room is tight, so think-tank Verité urges non‑debt options—time‑bound solidarity levies, better excise indexation and grant mobilisation—to avoid squeezing growth. The political economy test now is to pair quick wins (bridges, rail cutovers, cash‑for‑work) with transparent, digital delivery and livelihood recovery for farmers and SMEs, not just concrete. 🛠️

## 2. Education reforms become a test of consent over decree

The plan to refresh curricula has morphed into a political brawl. Protesters led by Wimal Weerawansa demand the resignation of Prime Minister and Education Minister Harini Amarasuriya; a no‑confidence debate is slated for late January. The Government has delayed Grade 6 changes to 2027 and promised state-funded classroom tech, while Tamil party ITAK declined to back the no‑confidence move, citing lack of evidence. Winning back teachers and parents—by phasing pilots, publishing modules, and fixing process errors—matters more than winning a vote. Reform by debate, not diktat, is the only durable route. 📚

## 3. Markets rally and banks bulk up as 2026 opens—but fundamentals still matter

The Colombo Stock Exchange broke its ASPI record (23,708.70) and posted heavy turnovers, buoyed by lower yields and banking optimism. Nations Trust Bank earned an upgrade on strong lending and a planned HSBC retail acquisition; Commercial Bank topped SME lending for a fifth year. Yet rates have ticked up and the rupee remains under pressure. To sustain animal spirits, Sri Lanka needs cleaner balance sheets, predictable regulation and better tax administration rather than new levies—so the system raises revenue by service, not fear. 📈

## 4. Energy costs are driven by thermal, but policy focus remains fuzzy

Storage-aided renewables could cut the bill, yet planning still lags technology. In 2023–24, thermal generation supplied roughly half of electricity but accounted for about four‑fifths of cost; renewables delivered the rest at a fraction of spend. Meanwhile, a contentious coal tender has triggered allegations of sub‑par calorific value and higher burn rates at Norochcholai; the Government awaits tests from an accredited lab. On the upside, the President inaugurated two wind projects in Mannar. The draft electricity policy needs crisper incentives for storage and flexibility, clear curtailment rules, and a transparent bridge from feed‑in tariffs to auctions—without kneecapping rooftop solar or local SMEs. 🔌

## 5. India leans into delivery: grid link, Trincomalee and rail repairs advance

New Delhi’s envoy says the India–Sri Lanka power grid interconnection has cleared engineering hurdles, with financing next; talks on a phased Trincomalee energy hub continue. India also rushed a $5m grant to repair Northern rail lines after Ditwah. A more sensitive file is land connectivity: India awaits Colombo’s response to a detailed project report request, which the envoy says originated in Sri Lanka. Any such project would demand full disclosure and robust public consent. Sri Lanka’s value proposition remains neutrality—hosting rival capital while taking none’s side. 🤝

## 6. Justice and integrity are under the microscope

The Prime Minister told Parliament that 65 cases withdrawn under previous governments have been re‑filed. Yet headlines also featured a military intelligence officer, charged in the disappearance of journalist Prageeth Ekneligoda, reportedly promoted—sparking protests by the family and rights groups. Meanwhile, anti‑corruption campaigners pressed for clarity on alleged coal procurement irregularities. Credibility will rest on equal rules: insulating investigators, publishing procurement tests, and halting sensitive promotions pending trial outcomes. ⚖️

## 7. Digital rails before new taxes: fix how citizens pay and move

Policy wins are coming from plumbing, not policy papers. The Transport Ministry rolled out a smart bus ticketing system; the Motor Traffic Department made Taxpayer Identification Numbers mandatory for most vehicle registrations and transfers; Fintech leaders backed a three‑year target to cut cash-in‑circulation-to‑GDP from 4.5% to 3.5% and called for a unified fintech regulator. The revenue frontier is administration: UK‑ and UAE‑style e‑filing, TIN coverage, API‑ready laws, and AI‑assisted, risk‑based audits will raise compliance faster than tinkering with rates. Smarter roads—well‑signposted speed cameras and digital enforcement—can also save lives and money. 💳

## 8. AI and skills: get on the bus—ethically and at speed

Educators and industry agree: artificial intelligence is already shaping classrooms and clinics. Webinars urged elective pilots, teacher upskilling, assessment redesign and safeguards against misuse. The EU‑funded ENSURE project, led by SLIIT with local and European partners, targets NetZero skills in storage, smart grids and hydrogen; COYLE is bringing Harvard’s Boris Groysberg to coach CEOs on cohesion and foresight. The prize is productivity; the risk is hype without guardrails. 🤖

## 9. A public development bank could anchor resilient growth

Sri Lanka’s past development banks (DFCC, NDB) drifted into commercial banking; the case grows for a new public development bank to fund long‑tenor, concessional, project‑based lending outside the Western Province and into value chains. Post‑Ditwah reconstruction, SME modernisation and regional industrialisation need patient capital that commercial lenders shun. Private ingenuity is not absent—local firms are piloting sustainable packaging, intrapreneurship and large‑scale rooftop solar—but a national balance‑sheet backstop would multiply impact. 🏦

## 10. Culture and cohesion bookend a bruising week

Thai Pongal brought the President to Jaffna and Mannar’s turbines to life, while civil groups staged a ‘Pongal for rights’ for the Malaiyaha Tamil community, the worst hit by landslides. The WAKFS Board urged fact‑based scrutiny; The HALO Trust named a Sri Lankan media figure as ambassador for mine action; solidarity events amplified Palestinian voices. Nation‑building is not a memo; it is ritual, rights and rebuilding—done together. 🛕

## Model Prompt (for [gpt-5](https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-5))

```
You will be given several Sri Lankan news articles from the past week. Write a concise, cohesive editorial digest in the style of The Economist, but output it as a JSON list. Each list element must be a dict of the form:

{
  "title": "...",
  "body": "..."
}

Titles should be written as crisp, fully informative sentences. Follow each title with a short paragraph in "body" that adds context and nuance without bullets.

Go beyond summary and analyse how the week’s developments fit together. Use emojis purposefully, and explain any non-obvious acronyms or jargon. Stick strictly to verifiable facts.
```
